title: Safety, Liquidity, and the Natural Rate of Interest

source.name: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

summary: This Federal Reserve Bank of New York Staff Report (Number 812) investigates why U.S. interest rates are low. The authors conclude that rates are depressed primarily due to increased safety and liquidity premiums since the late 1990s, with slower economic growth as a secondary factor. The paper uses two complementary methodologies: a time-series model of Treasury/corporate yields and a medium-scale DSGE model.
key_points: Authors: Marco Del Negro, Domenico Giannone, Marc Giannoni, and Andrea Tambalotti
The safety and liquidity premium increased since the late 1990s, driving down interest rates
Slower economic growth contributed to lower rates to a lesser extent
Research used a flexible time-series model of Treasury and corporate yields, inflation, and long-term survey expectations
A medium-scale dynamic stochastic general equilibrium (DSGE) model was used as a complementary approach
Document is available only in PDF format, 80 pages
JEL classifications: C11, C32, C54, E43, E44
why_it_matters: Understanding the drivers of the natural rate of interest has implications for monetary policy calibration. If low rates are driven by structural increases in safety/liquidity premiums rather than weak growth, this suggests different policy responses. This is a May 2017 Federal Reserve research paper — note that it predates COVID-19, the 2022-2024 inflation surge, and significant shifts in Fed policy. As a historical research record, it provides context on pre-pandemic thinking about interest rate dynamics but may have limited direct relevance to current market conditions.
macro_context: This NY Fed Staff Report (Number 812) provides a framework for understanding the natural rate of interest as driven by safety/liquidity premiums and trend growth. The finding that premium shifts (rather than pure growth) explain low rates has implications for monetary policy interpretation. Published May 2017 — this analysis predates the post-COVID inflation surge, the 2022-2024 tightening cycle, and subsequent rate environment. Historical research record only.
